subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Bachelor’s Degree, completed at age 22
Major likely in Criminal Justice, Public Administration, or a related field
Received additional on-the-job training specific to emergency dispatch procedures
Attended mandatory state-level certification for 911 operators
Participated in regular continuing education courses for dispatchers

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Job title: Emergency Dispatcher (also known as 911 Operator or Telecommunicator)
Responsible for answering emergency calls and dispatching police, fire, EMS, and rescue units
Handles administrative calls as well as 911 emergencies
Runs background checks, vehicle records, and criminal histories as part of role
Acts as a communication hub between the public and emergency services

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the Public Safety and Emergency Services sector
Employed by a government or municipal agency
Role is central to law enforcement, fire, and medical emergency operations
Contributes directly to public safety infrastructure

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Works at a medium-sized emergency communications center
Team consists of approximately 10–30 dispatchers across shifts
Operates in a 24/7 high-pressure environment
Workplace features multiple workstations with specialized equipment

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Government-operated emergency communications center
Serves local city or county jurisdiction(s)
Supports police, fire, EMS, and rescue units
Handles both emergency and non-emergency public safety calls

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 3–5 years of experience as an emergency dispatcher
Started in the role shortly after completing college
Has worked in the same center since entry into the profession
Accumulated experience across multiple types of emergency situations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Considered early-to-mid career, not entry-level
Holds a full-time, line dispatcher position
May act as a peer mentor to newer hires
Not yet in a supervisory or lead dispatcher role

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Answers incoming 911 and administrative phone calls
Dispatches and coordinates police, fire, EMS, and rescue units
Operates and logs incidents in the CAD (Computer-Aided Dispatch) system
Runs background and license checks via NCIC
Maintains ongoing radio communication with field units
Documents call details and actions taken for each incident

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends approximately 40% of shift answering phones
Allocates about 30% of time managing radio traffic and dispatching units
Devotes about 20% of time to entering and updating CAD records
Uses remaining 10% for running checks, messaging officers, and administrative duties

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Operates independently during most calls and dispatch scenarios
Decides which emergency units to send based on call details and protocol
Exercises judgment when prioritizing simultaneous emergencies
Consults supervisors only for highly unusual or escalated situations
Has authority to initiate backup or additional resources as needed

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Answering and triaging straightforward emergency calls
Dispatching routine incidents such as traffic stops or minor accidents
Logging call information into CAD during or immediately after calls
Using Solicom and CAD software interfaces for standard operations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Handling emotionally charged or high-stress emergency calls
Extracting accurate information from confused or distressed callers
Coordinating simultaneous multi-agency responses (e.g., police and EMS)
Running detailed background checks with limited or unclear information

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Entering routine details into the CAD system for each call
Running standard license, vehicle, and firearms checks via NCIC
Documenting officer status updates and location changes
Transferring non-emergency administrative calls

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Finds engaging: solving complex dispatch scenarios or critical emergencies
Feels rewarded by successfully helping callers in distress
Enjoys the fast-paced, unpredictable nature of emergency response
Finds entering repetitive data or handling routine administrative calls tedious

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Dealing with callers who provide excessive or irrelevant information
Navigating multiple software systems that may not always integrate smoothly
Limited search capabilities in NCIC when only partial information is available
Time pressure when handling overlapping calls and radio traffic

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Most productive and accurate when highly engaged with urgent calls
Enjoyment increases with skill mastery, especially in high-stress scenarios
Tedious, repetitive tasks can feel draining and reduce perceived productivity
Prefers work that balances challenge with the opportunity to help others

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Open to using new technologies that improve workflow efficiency
Has a positive attitude toward digital tools and software
Values hands-on training for effective learning
Seeks practical relevance before adopting new systems

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Considers self quick to learn new dispatch and communication tools
Regularly adapts to updates in CAD and phone systems
Confident navigating multi-system workstations
Occasionally needs refreshers for infrequently used functions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to attend mandatory and optional training sessions
Prefers short, focused training modules over lengthy seminars
Will invest time if new tools directly reduce manual workload
Values peer-led training or shadowing for practical learning

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by clear demonstrations of improved speed or accuracy
Workload during busy shifts can be a barrier to extra training
Skeptical of tech perceived as redundant or overly complicated
Peer recommendations influence willingness to learn new tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers who embrace new tech make adoption easier and more appealing
Management support and clear communication increase willingness
Relies on experienced colleagues for practical tips during rollout
Team consensus plays a role in tool acceptance

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses Solicom for integrating phone systems and managing emergency/admin calls
Operates CAD (Computer-Aided Dispatch) system for call logging and resource tracking
Accesses NCIC for running driver’s licenses, tags, guns, and articles checks
Utilizes radio dispatch hardware and messaging features in CAD

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Relies on multi-line phone consoles and headsets
Uses dedicated radio hardware for communication with field units
Employs manual call logs and printed reference materials as backups
Keeps quick-access directories for agency and county contacts

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Experienced with industry-standard CAD/dispatch software
Trained in use of NCIC for national database searches
Familiar with standard radio communication protocols
Understands security and privacy standards for public safety data

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasionally experiences lag or mismatches between Solicom and CAD data
NCIC queries sometimes require manual entry due to lack of integration
Switching between multiple platforms can slow response during high call volume
Integration with neighboring county systems can be inconsistent

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Regularly uses large language models (LLMs) outside of work for personal purposes
Understands basic AI/ML terminology such as 'model,' 'training,' and 'prompt'
Aware of general AI capabilities, especially in language and text analysis
Follows news and updates on AI trends relevant to professional and personal life

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Has not used AI tools, including LLMs, for any work-related activities
Primary dispatch and records systems do not currently include AI-driven features
No AI-based transcription or voice analytics in current workflow

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses LLMs regularly outside of work for general information and learning
No work-related AI tool usage to date
Sees potential for AI in automating routine data entry or information retrieval tasks
Currently relies on human judgment and standard software for all critical functions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: Has not experienced AI successes or failures in the context of daily dispatch work
Personal AI use has shown that LLMs can be helpful for drafting text or summarizing info
Skeptical about AI's ability to handle high-emotion, high-stakes emergency calls effectively
Recognizes that AI can sometimes misunderstand nuance or urgency in human communication

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: No formal organizational training on AI tools provided to dispatchers
Peers have not widely adopted or discussed AI use in the workplace
Occasional informal discussions about AI trends, but no hands-on exposure
Would consider training if AI tools were introduced to dispatch workflow

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers independent decision-making during urgent calls
Would favor step-by-step AI assistance for routine or repetitive tasks
Values autonomy in critical scenarios where human judgment is paramount
Open to AI collaboration if it increases speed without impeding workflow

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI best suited as an assistant for background checks and record retrieval
Prefers AI in a supportive, non-decision-making role
Would utilize AI as a co-worker for data-heavy tasks but not for caller triage
Unlikely to delegate high-stakes decisions to AI without human oversight

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Willing to try AI-driven workflow enhancements if proven to be reliable
Open to pilot programs or controlled rollouts before full adoption
Requires clear demonstration of AI’s benefit to consider workflow changes
Resistant to replacing core human judgment with automation in emergencies

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Familiar and comfortable with conversational AI interfaces from personal LLM use
Would prefer concise, command-based AI input for rapid task execution
Recognizes value in both conversational and command-based modes depending on context
In high-stress situations, efficiency outweighs interface style

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Many tasks require reading caller emotions and discerning unspoken urgency
Dispatch decisions often depend on local protocols and nuanced context
Accurate triage demands experience with human behavior under stress
Knowledge of local geography and agency procedures is critical

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Low trust in AI for making critical, time-sensitive dispatch decisions
Cautiously open to AI handling background checks and data entry
Requires human verification of any AI-generated recommendations
Believes AI could assist but not replace core responsibilities

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI automation for repetitive, data-driven tasks (e.g., license checks)
Human intervention necessary for caller triage and resource prioritization
Envisions hybrid workflows where AI augments but does not supplant dispatchers
Manual override is essential for any automated process

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for backend administrative processing
Finds high automation threatening for direct caller interaction or critical dispatch
Automation must not delay or complicate urgent response actions
Transparency and clear override options mitigate perceived threats

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes core interpersonal and crisis management skills will remain essential
Expects some technical skills (data entry, record management) may become less critical
Anticipates need for new skills in operating and supervising AI systems
Views adaptability as increasingly important in evolving environments

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Learning to interpret and validate AI-generated suggestions
Developing troubleshooting skills for AI-integrated dispatch systems
Becoming familiar with ethical guidelines for AI use in public safety
Potential responsibility for training peers on new AI tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Minimal concern about full replacement due to human judgment demands
Some concern about automation of data entry and administrative duties
Sees AI as a tool to assist rather than fully replace dispatchers
Wary of job reduction if AI advances without appropriate checks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Believes manual checks are non-negotiable for critical decisions
Would only accept AI-driven processes with robust override features
Prefers systems that highlight but do not automatically act on AI suggestions
Supports clear audit trails for AI-influenced actions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: No significant policy changes on AI adoption observed to date
Management has not formally introduced AI initiatives in dispatch operations
Team discussions on AI are informal and speculative
Would expect formal policy updates if AI tools are piloted or deployed

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Sees AI as beneficial in many industries for efficiency and data handling
Skeptical about AI’s ability to replace human empathy and judgment in emergency services
Believes AI can enhance but not fully automate public safety roles
Supports responsible and transparent AI integration

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Values transparency and explainability in AI recommendations
Worried about potential bias or error in AI-driven decisions
Believes human oversight is necessary to prevent ethical lapses
Concerned about privacy and data security in AI-enabled dispatch systems

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Automated transcription and summarization of calls for CAD entry
AI-powered search for incomplete or partial information in databases
Real-time caller sentiment analysis as an assistive tool
Predictive analytics for resource deployment during peak periods

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects gradual adoption of AI for support tasks, not core dispatch
Foresees increased need for training in supervising AI systems
Believes human dispatchers will remain essential for complex emergencies
Anticipates more hybrid workflows blending manual and automated processes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Pilot AI tools in low-risk, high-volume tasks before wider rollout
Provide comprehensive training and clear documentation for any new AI systems
Establish guidelines for human oversight and intervention in AI-driven workflows
Solicit dispatcher feedback before and during AI deployment
